concepts.match_reason: A decoder-only Transformer is the whole architecture using only decoder-style causal self-attention; candidate 0 is just the decoder stack component and includes cross-attention, so it is not the same concept.

concepts.name: Adaptive input
concepts.description: A parameterization for word embeddings that allocates modeling capacity unevenly across the vocabulary. It typically uses different embedding sizes or transforms for frequent and infrequent tokens to improve efficiency.

concepts.name: Adaptive softmax
concepts.description: A softmax approximation that reduces computation for large vocabularies by partitioning words into frequency-based clusters. It speeds up training and inference while preserving good probability estimates for common and rare tokens.
concepts.match_reason: Adaptive softmax is an output-layer approximation for large-vocabulary softmax, while adaptive input is an input embedding parameterization; they are related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: Negative log-likelihood
concepts.description: A loss function that measures how much probability a model assigns to observed data. In language modeling, it is minimized so that the model increases the likelihood of the training corpus.

concepts.name: Training set
concepts.description: The collection of examples used to fit a model's parameters. It provides the data over which statistics, representations, or caches may be built for later use.

concepts.name: Prior context
concepts.description: The sequence of preceding tokens available to a language model when predicting the next token. Longer prior context gives the model more surrounding information for contextual disambiguation.

concepts.name: FAISS index
concepts.description: An approximate nearest-neighbor search index built with FAISS for efficient similarity lookup over large vector collections. It supports fast retrieval by organizing vectors into clusters and searching only a subset of them.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a specific FAISS-built index structure, while the candidate is the FAISS library itself; they are related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: Cluster centroid
concepts.description: A representative vector for a cluster of data points, typically used in vector quantization or approximate nearest-neighbor search. Centroids help reduce search cost by partitioning the space into regions that can be searched selectively.

concepts.name: Quantization
concepts.description: A compression method that maps high-precision vectors to lower-precision or compact representations. In vector search systems, it reduces memory usage and can speed up similarity computation at some cost in accuracy.

concepts.name: Continuous cache model
concepts.description: A cache-based language modeling technique that predicts tokens by retrieving similar recent hidden states from earlier positions in the same document. It improves prediction by exploiting short-range repetition and topical locality in text.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a specific continuous-cache variant that retrieves similar recent hidden states, while the candidate is the broader cache model in general.

concepts.name: Vanilla language model
concepts.description: A standard language model that predicts tokens directly from learned parameters without retrieval augmentation or external memory. It serves as a baseline for comparison against enhanced language modeling methods.
concepts.match_reason: “Vanilla language model” means a plain baseline LM without retrieval/external memory; none of the candidates denote that exact concept, and the closest ones are either a training regime or a broader/neural LM variant.

concepts.name: Transformer language model layer
concepts.description: A layer in a Transformer-based language model that combines attention, a feed-forward transformation, and normalization steps. It processes token representations to produce contextualized hidden states used for next-token prediction.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a specific Transformer language model layer, while the candidates are the whole model, the Transformer architecture, or a generic layer; none denotes the same concept.

concepts.name: In-domain language model
concepts.description: A language model trained on data from the same domain as the target evaluation data. Because its training distribution matches the target domain, it typically achieves lower perplexity than an out-of-domain model.

concepts.name: Quantized key
concepts.description: A compressed vector representation used to reduce memory usage and speed up similarity search. Quantization lowers precision, which can trade off accuracy for efficiency in nearest-neighbor retrieval.
concepts.match_reason: The candidate is the quantization method, while the incoming concept is the quantized vector representation produced by that method.

concepts.name: Full-precision key
concepts.description: A vector representation stored without aggressive compression or quantization. Using full precision can improve the accuracy of similarity computations in nearest-neighbor search.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is an uncompressed high-precision vector, while the candidates describe quantized/compressed lower-precision representations or the quantization process itself.

concepts.name: n-gram language model
concepts.description: A probabilistic language model that predicts the next token from a fixed-size context of previous tokens. It estimates local word-sequence statistics directly from observed n-gram counts or smoothed n-gram probabilities.
concepts.match_reason: An n-gram language model is a specific count-based model, while the candidates are broader language modeling concepts or different model types; none denotes that same concept.

concepts.name: learned representation function
concepts.description: A neural mapping that converts input contexts or examples into vector representations. These representations are designed so that similar contexts are placed near each other, enabling similarity-based retrieval or comparison.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a specific learned mapping/function that produces embeddings, while the candidate is the broader process of learning representations; they are related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: explicit memory
concepts.description: Information stored outside model parameters and accessed directly during prediction. In neural language modeling, this typically means a retrievable collection of stored examples or representations rather than knowledge encoded implicitly in weights.
concepts.match_reason: Explicit memory is the general idea of information stored outside parameters and accessed directly, while a cache model is only one specific memory mechanism for reusing recent states.

concepts.name: rare pattern
concepts.description: A sequence or structure that appears only infrequently in the data. Rare patterns are challenging for standard parametric models because they provide limited direct evidence during training.

concepts.name: factual knowledge
concepts.description: Information about real-world facts such as entities, properties, and relationships. In language modeling, it often appears as memorized or retrievable content rather than purely syntactic regularities.

concepts.name: near-duplicate sentence
concepts.description: A sentence that is almost the same as another sentence, differing only slightly in wording or punctuation. Such sentences can be highly useful for retrieval-based methods because they provide closely matching context for prediction.

concepts.name: overfitting
concepts.description: A situation in which a model fits the training data extremely well but performs worse on unseen data. It usually indicates that the model has captured idiosyncratic details of the training set rather than generalizable structure.

concepts.name: training loss
concepts.description: The objective value computed on the training set during optimization. Lower training loss means the model is fitting the observed data better, and a value near zero indicates near-perfect fit on the training examples.
